meta.title: Legora Interview Prep Deck
meta.subtitle: Reliable systems around probabilistic components
meta.interview: Greg Bell, Hiring Manager, May 8, 2026, 11:15 AM-12:00 PM EDT

html: Legora / Senior Software Engineer / Hiring Manager
Reliable systems around probabilistic components
Use this deck to rehearse platform judgment, end-to-end ownership, and trust-aware agent design for the May 8, 2026 interview with Greg Bell.
45 minutesEngineering HM11:15 AM-12:00 PM EDTNew York engineering hub
notes: Open with the stance, not generic AI enthusiasm.
Core claim: reliable AI systems need permissions, validation, observability, and workflow fit.

html: Why Legora, why this role

Positioning

I build reliable AI and data systems for high-stakes workflows. Legal AI is compelling because the model is only one component: the surrounding platform has to be source-grounded, permission-aware, auditable, and useful inside expert work.

Bridge

  • Frame Payments: document workflow, guardrails, human review.
  • JPMorgan: regulated enterprise reliability and observability.
  • Startup mode: ambiguity to shipped vertical slices.
Thesis: Legora's current product direction rewards engineers who can turn agent demos into dependable workflows.
notes: Avoid market hype. Make the answer about trustworthy systems around expert users.
Use the phrase: not demo-first prompting, but workflow-first systems engineering.

May 7, 2026

May 4, 2026

Apr 23, 2026
Use these as technical anchors: end-to-end workflow, verified evidence, structured legal data, real-time regulatory context.
notes: These signals are more useful than general company facts.
If Greg asks why Legora now, connect the releases to operating-system scope and integration depth.

html: Scale and trust posture

$100M+ ARRreported Apr 2, 2026
1,000+ customerslaw firms and in-house teams
$600M Series D$5.6B post-money valuation after extension
400 employeesfrom 40 over the past year

{
  "security_frame": {
    "authorize": "before retrieval",
    "isolate": ["tenant", "matter", "document_acl"],
    "trace": ["source_data", "prompt", "tool_call", "agent_action"],
    "approval": "lawyer for client-facing output"
  }
}
notes: The security page supports the vocabulary: ISO 42001, ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, GDPR, Zero Trust, Zanzibar-style access control, and traceability to source data and prompts.
Make scale a reliability argument, not a brag.

html: SOTA pattern map for legal agents

Workflow first

Use predictable workflows when the path is known: intake, classify, retrieve, draft, validate, approve.

Agents where useful

Use planner-worker behavior only when the work requires dynamic decomposition or tool choice.

Guardrail each boundary

Validate inputs, tool calls, outputs, citations, action permissions, and escalation state.

Trace everything

Keep a run record for prompts, retrieved sources, tool calls, handoffs, guardrails, and human edits.
Interview phrasing: I would start deterministic, add agent autonomy only where it buys workflow coverage, and keep every model step inspectable.
notes: This slide aligns with Anthropic's workflows-versus-agents framing and OpenAI's guardrails/tracing emphasis.
Use it to answer architecture questions without sounding framework-dependent.

html: 60-second pitch

I build production systems around probabilistic components in high-stakes environments.

My strongest work sits at the intersection of AI workflows, data-intensive systems, and platform reliability.

What makes Legora compelling is that the product is moving toward an operating system for legal work, where trust, deep integrations, and end-to-end workflow execution matter.
notes: Keep this under a minute.
Land on ownership, tradeoff clarity, observable systems, and expert workflows.

html: Choose the story by signal

Frame Payments

AI systems, document workflows, guardrails, human-in-loop.

JPMorgan

Reliability, regulated systems, observability, enterprise users.

Startup mode

Ambiguity, autonomy, product engineering, execution.

Ownership or AI systems: start with Frame Payments.

Scale or enterprise trust: use JPMorgan.

Ambiguity or speed: use startup mode.
notes: Never invent metrics. Say materially reduced repeated follow-up loops unless a specific number is verified.
Use the r-stack pacing only if the conversation needs rehearsal, not in a fast interview.

html: Frame Payments: agentic compliance workflow

Claim

I can build AI systems where correctness, traceability, and workflow fit matter more than flashy generation.

Shape

  1. Business pain and document back-and-forth.
  2. Structured extraction and explicit failure states.
  3. Human escalation for ambiguous or high-risk cases.
  4. Inspectable outputs, source support, validation failures.

Legora map

Document complexity, high-trust output, auditability, expert review, workflow compression.

The model did not solve the problem alone; the system design did.
notes: Default first story. Answer shape: problem, constraints, architecture, validation, impact.
Stress that the safety boundary is product and platform design, not a prompt.

html: JPMorgan: regulated enterprise data infrastructure

• Built and maintained data infrastructure with clear downstream interfaces.
  • Improved signal quality instead of only adding alerts or dashboards.
  • Handled latency, failure isolation, maintainability, and observability tradeoffs.
  • Communicated complexity across stakeholder groups without hiding the risk.
Use for: reliability, scale, expert users, regulated enterprise credibility.
notes: Replace with exact verified metric if available.
Otherwise frame the result as reduced operational noise and improved adoption across internal users.

html: Hard questions: answer as a system

How do you reduce hallucinations?

Treat model output as untrusted until validated. Ground retrieval on authorized sources, constrain outputs with schemas, verify citations and business rules, expose uncertainty, and route sensitive actions through human approval.

How do you prevent permission leakage?

Authorize before retrieval. Normalize identity, tenant, matter, role, and document ACLs into query-time filters across keyword and vector retrieval.

How do you debug an unsupported claim?

Trace the request, retrieved sources, permission filters, prompts, tool calls, citations, validators, and final user edits. Turn the failure into an eval.
notes: The stronger phrase: I want a reproducible trace and regression test, not a one-off explanation.
If pressed on model choice, pivot to eval-driven routing rather than favorite-model opinions.

html: System design prompt

A lawyer receives a contract redline or client request. Design a system that reviews changes against matter context, firm playbooks, precedent, and authoritative sources; proposes a response; and routes it for lawyer approval before delivery.
permissionscontextplannertoolsvalidationapprovaldeliveryevals
Start with the risk boundary and workflow. Do not make the agent an opaque transaction.
notes: Use this if Greg asks architecture. Start with tenants, matters, document versions, and human approval.
Make the workflow inspectable before adding model sophistication.

html: Architecture diagram
Zoom or open the lightbox when rehearsing the left-to-right walkthrough.
notes: Walk left to right, then bottom row back to trace and evals.
Mention async jobs, idempotency, retries, and compensating actions if asked about partial failure.

html: Permission and trace contract

Runtime contract

  • No retrieval without tenant, matter, role, and document ACL.
  • No client-facing output without citation validation and lawyer approval.
  • No silent tool action without idempotency key and audit event.
{
  "run_id": "matter-review-042",
  "allowed_sources": ["matter_docs", "playbook", "authorized_research"],
  "blocked": ["cross_matter_vector_hit"],
  "human_gate": "required_before_send"
}
notes: This is the slide to show engineering taste.
The system should make invalid states hard to represent, not just hope the model follows instructions.

html: Failure modes and gates

Failure modes

  • Retrieval leakage across matters or tenants.
  • Citations that do not support the generated claim.
  • Silent truncation or missed document versions.
  • Integration failure after partial workflow completion.
  • Overconfident output when sources conflict.

Eval gates

  • Permission leakage: deterministic ACL fixtures, 100% pass.
  • Citation support: source-span verifier plus expert sample.
  • Review burden: lawyer rating and edit distance trend down.
  • Completion quality: outcome state meets workflow SLA.
  • Tool safety: guardrails around every mutating function call.
notes: Every system answer needs a failure mode.
Prefer eval language over vibes.

html: Evals ladder for a legal workflow

Unitschemas, parsers, ACL filters, citation parser
Golden mattersexpert-labeled requests with expected sources and outcomes
Adversarialcross-matter leakage, prompt injection, conflicting authority
Onlinereview burden, edit distance, escalation rate, completion SLA
Failure becomes data: trace the miss, label it, add it to the eval suite, and prevent recurrence.
notes: Show a ladder from deterministic tests to expert evaluation to production telemetry.
If asked about velocity, say the eval harness is what lets the team move faster without hiding risk.

html: First 90 days

0-30

Map product surface, agent runtime, integration boundaries, on-call posture, and the eval stack. Ship one small reliability improvement.

31-60

Own a vertical slice: permissioned retrieval or validation path through delivery, with traces and regression coverage.

61-90

Broaden leverage: remove a platform bottleneck, codify a reusable workflow pattern, and document the operating standard.
This is a senior-engineer answer: learn fast, ship safely, then scale the pattern.
notes: Use this near the end if the interviewer asks about ramp or ownership.
Make it concrete and humble: learn the system, then increase leverage.

html: Smart questions to ask

1. For the NYC engineering hub, what platform or product bottlenecks should senior engineers remove first?
  2. How is ownership split across agent runtime, integrations, retrieval, product surfaces, and infrastructure?
  3. What is a high-quality agentic workflow: accuracy, latency, adoption, review burden, completion rate?
  4. Where are the hardest reliability problems now: permissions, long-running workflows, model variability, integrations, document scale, or evals?
notes: Pick two based on what Greg emphasizes.
End with what a great first 90 days would look like.

html: Close with the thesis
I am strongest where systems reliability and AI workflow depth have to meet.
Legora's current direction is exactly that: legal-domain agents, permissioned context, integrations, validation, and expert approval inside the flow of work.
notes: Make the close crisp.
Tie your strongest evidence back to aOS, Qura, Graceview, Everlaw, and the security model.
